question: A Java job worker is configured with `maxJobsActive = 10`. After it has just activated 10 jobs from a single poll, at what point does it issue its next poll for more jobs?

options.text: When the count of unhandled jobs drops below 30% of `maxJobsActive` rounded up — in this case below 3.
Immediately after handling any one job, so the in-flight buffer is always topped up to `maxJobsActive`.
Only after all 10 activated jobs have been handled, never before.
After a fixed `pollInterval`, regardless of how many jobs are still in flight.

a.text: Each time a job is handled, the worker checks whether the unhandled count has dropped below 30% of `maxJobsActive` (rounded up). The first time that condition is met, it polls for more — for `maxJobsActive = 10` that threshold is 3.

b.text: The worker does not re-poll after every single handled job; the docs describe a 30%-of-`maxJobsActive` threshold instead of one-at-a-time topping-up.

c.text: Waiting until all jobs are handled would leave the worker idle right before re-polling; the threshold deliberately tops up before the buffer empties.

d.text: `pollInterval` governs how long the worker waits when the previous poll returned no jobs (or failed). After a successful poll, the 30% threshold drives the next poll, not `pollInterval`.

explanation: The Java job worker pre-fetches `maxJobsActive` jobs and then polls again only when the number of unhandled jobs drops below 30% of `maxJobsActive`, rounded up. For `maxJobsActive = 10` that threshold is 3 unhandled jobs — matching the docs' worked example (with `maxJobsActive = 3` the threshold is 1). `pollInterval` applies to the empty-result and error paths, not to mid-batch top-ups.
